title: Tuning the low-energy band structure in twisted bilayer WSe2
authors: T. -H. -Y. Vu, O. J. Clark, N. H. Jo, J. Blyth, Q. Li, C. Jozwiak, A. Bostwick, J. B. Muir, L. Jia, J. A. Davis, I. Di Bernardo, A. Grubisic Cabo, K. Xing, W. Zhao, S. H. Ryu, S. H. Lee, Z. Mao, K. Watanabe, T. Taniguchi, B. A. Chambers, S. L. Harmer, E. Rotenberg, M. S. Fuhrer, M. T. Edmonds
abstract: Tuning the electronic structures of two-dimensional (2D) material-based heterostructures is of crucial importance for their use in functional next-generation electronics. Here, through angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy with nanoscale spatial resolution (nano-ARPES), we systematically track the evolution of the near-Fermi-level electronic structure of bilayer WSe2 over a large range of twist angle. While the momentum positioning of the valence band maxima is independent of twist angle, we find that the energetic separation between the hole bands at the K point of the Brillouin zone and the higher binding-energy hole band at Γ can be varied in excess of 100 meV. We explore the mechanisms underpinning this evolution and discuss the implications for tuning both the size of the band gaps, and the efficiency of the spin-dependent electron-phonon coupling channels in homobilayer transition metal dichalcogenide devices.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the twist angle in bilayer WSe2 can be used to precisely tune the energy separation between different hole bands. This tuning capability allows for the control of band gaps and electron-phonon coupling efficiency in TMD-based electronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to understand how the twist angle in bilayer WSe2 tunes the low-energy electronic band structure, specifically the energy separation between the K and Gamma points.
main_result: The momentum positioning of the valence band maxima is independent of the twist angle, but the energetic separation between the K-point and Gamma-point hole bands can be tuned by over 100 meV via the twist angle.
method: The researchers used nano-angle-resolved photoemission spectroscopy (nano-ARPES) with nanoscale spatial resolution to track electronic structure evolution.
model_or_system: The physical system is a 2H-structured twisted bilayer WSe2 (a transition-metal dichalcogenide) van der Waals heterostructure.
key_observables: Momentum positioning of valence band maxima, energy separation between K and Gamma hole bands, band dispersion, and spin-orbit splitting.
important_parameters: Twist angle (ranging from 0 to 120 degrees), binding energy, and interlayer hopping channels.
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the observed band shifts are driven by interlayer hopping of d-orbitals rather than moire-induced flat bands or correlated phases.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the nano-ARPES setup; Figure 3 shows the K-Gamma energy separation findings; Supplementary Figure S1 shows band dispersions and Energy Distribution Curves (EDCs) with fitted peaks.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of band tuning in TMDs, describes the experimental fabrication and nano-ARPES setup, presents the systematic measurement of band evolution across twist angles, discusses the underlying mechanism of interlayer hopping, and concludes with implications for electron-phonon coupling.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
